name: Pannell Ridge
short_description: Pannell Ridge in Bedford County offers rockhounders access to Paleozoic limestone exposures where chert, quartz, and chalcedony nodules weather out naturally. The gentle slope terrain makes this a ...
description:
Pannell Ridge sits within the Highland Rim region of central Tennessee, an area characterized by rolling limestone plateaus and ridges that developed during the Paleozoic Era. The site is known for surface collection of secondary minerals including chert nodules, quartz fragments, and chalcedony specimens that accumulate along ridge faces and drainage areas as the host limestone naturally weathers. The elevation of approximately 990 feet provides moderate relief with gentle slopes that make access relatively straightforward compared to more rugged Tennessee rockhounding sites.

The geology of this location reflects the carbonate platform environment that dominated Tennessee during the Ordovician and Silurian periods. Chert nodules formed as silica-rich fluids replaced limestone in horizontal bedding planes, creating the characteristic gray and tan nodules that weather out and collect along hillsides. Collectors often find small to medium-sized specimens suitable for tumbling, cutting, or display purposes.

Rockhounding at Pannell Ridge is best conducted by walking ridge faces and natural drainage channels where specimens naturally accumulate. The gentle slope terrain means minimal scrambling is required, though sturdy footwear is recommended due to loose chert fragments and uneven footing. Most collecting requires only hand tools - a small pick, chisel, and collection bag are sufficient.

The site offers a good introduction to Tennessee's Paleozoic geology and provides an opportunity to understand how secondary minerals form in carbonate-dominated terrains. Surface collecting without excavation disturbs minimal area while yielding quality specimens.
history: Pannell Ridge sits within the Highland Rim, a physiographic region shaped by erosion of Paleozoic limestone and dolostone formations. The chert and chalcedony nodules found here formed during the Ordovician through Silurian periods (approximately 485-420 million years ago) as silica-rich groundwater replaced portions of the original limestone, creating resistant nodules that now weather out as the host rock erodes.
tips: Focus your search on ridge faces and natural drainage channels where chert nodules naturally accumulate and are exposed - avoid heavy digging and rely instead on surface collecting. Look for gray, tan, or cream-colored nodules with a waxy luster, ranging from fist-sized down to pebble-sized pieces; the translucent varieties are prized for tumbling. Bring a 5-pound hammer or chisel to test nodule quality before collection, and check specimens under bright light to identify chalcedony veining. Visit after heavy rain when freshly weathered material is most accessible.
terrain_notes: The site sits at approximately 990 feet elevation with gentle to moderate slopes across limestone ridge terrain. Vegetation includes mixed hardwood forest and open ridge areas. Walking distance from parking to productive collecting areas is typically 0.25 to 0.75 miles. Footing is uneven with loose chert fragments and hidden rocks - sturdy hiking boots are essential. The ridge offers natural vistas of the surrounding Highland Rim landscape.
best_season: October - April. Fall through early spring provides the most comfortable collecting conditions, with moderate temperatures and lower humidity. This season also follows summer and fall rains that expose fresh weathered material. Summer heat and humidity make midday collecting uncomfortable, while winter offers clear skies and good visibility across ridge exposures.
written_directions: From Shelbyville, take US 41A north approximately 8 miles toward Manchester. Turn east onto CR 303 (Old Chapel Road) and continue for approximately 2 miles. Look for a small gravel pullout on the north side of the road near ridge-top elevations. Park safely off the road and access the ridge on foot by walking upslope through open woodland to the main chert-bearing exposures approximately 0.25 miles from the road.
road_conditions: County road (CR 303) is passable by passenger vehicle. Surface is paved with gravel sections. Standard clearance sufficient.
parking_notes: Small gravel pullout off CR 303 with space for 2-3 vehicles. No formal parking area; pull completely off roadway.
collection_rules: Personal use surface collection only. Respect private property boundaries. Do not dig into bedrock; collect naturally weathered specimens from the surface. Remove all trash. No motorized equipment. Do not disturb vegetation or animal habitats. Leave the site better than you found it.
rules:

Respect private property boundaries
Do not dig into bedrock - surface collection only
Remove all trash

nearest_services: Shelbyville, approximately 10 miles south. Full services including gas, restaurants, and grocery stores available.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat to gently sloping ridge terrain accessible to most hikers in reasonable physical condition. Gravel pullout parking and walking path through open woodland to collecting areas. No wheelchair access to ridge face exposures. Uneven footing with loose chert fragments and hidden rocks - sturdy hiking boots required. Minimal scrambling needed.

faq.question: What gemstones can I find rockhounding at Pannell Ridge in Tennessee?
faq.answer: Pannell Ridge is primarily known for chert, quartz, and chalcedony nodules that weather out of the Paleozoic limestone bedrock. Specimens range from small pebbles to fist-sized nodules in gray, tan, and cream colors. Some pieces display translucent chalcedony veining suitable for tumbling or cutting into cabochons.

faq.question: Is rockhounding at Pannell Ridge free, or do I need to pay a fee?
faq.answer: Pannell Ridge is free public rockhounding with no fees, permits, or reservations required. Personal use surface collection is allowed on this site. Simply park safely off the county road and begin collecting naturally weathered specimens from the ridge and drainage areas.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound at Pannell Ridge near Shelbyville?
faq.answer: October through April offers the best rockhounding conditions at Pannell Ridge. Fall and winter provide cooler temperatures, lower humidity, and better visibility. This period also follows seasonal rains that expose fresh weathered material. Summer heat and humidity make midday collecting uncomfortable.

faq.question: How difficult is rockhounding at Pannell Ridge for beginners?
faq.answer: Pannell Ridge is an excellent beginner-friendly site with easy, gentle-slope terrain and straightforward surface collecting. No heavy equipment or technical skills are required - a small hammer and collection bag are sufficient. Families with children ages 6 and up can enjoy collecting weathered chert and quartz specimens safely.

faq.question: What tools and equipment do I need to rockhound at Pannell Ridge Tennessee?
faq.answer: Bring sturdy hiking boots, a small 5-pound hammer or chisel, a flathead chisel for testing nodules, a collection bag, and water. A hand lens helps identify chalcedony veining. A GPS device is helpful but not required. Gloves protect hands from sharp chert fragments.

faq.question: Can I dig or excavate at Pannell Ridge, or is surface collection only?
faq.answer: Surface collection only is permitted at Pannell Ridge. Do not dig into bedrock or use motorized equipment. Collect naturally weathered chert and quartz nodules that have accumulated on the ridge surface and in drainage channels. This approach preserves the site while still yielding quality specimens.

meta_description: Find chert, quartz, and chalcedony at Pannell Ridge in Bedford County, Tennessee. Free public rockhounding on gentle ridge terrain near Shelbyville. Easy for beginners.
